abstract: We present the pressure evolution of charge density wave (CDW) order and the onset of superconductivity (SC) in 1T-VSe2 single crystals, determined from resistance and magnetoresistance measurements under high quasihydrostatic pressure. With increasing pressure the CDW is initially strengthened: the CDW ordering temperature (∼100 K at ambient pressure) rises only slightly up to 5 GPa. Above 5 GPa the CDW-like resistive anomaly shifts rapidly to higher temperature, reaching a characteristic temperature of ∼290 K at 14.2 GPa. Upon further compression the CDW-related resistance anomaly is suppressed abruptly while the sample becomes markedly more metallic (as evidenced by an increased RRR), and superconductivity appears near ∼15 GPa with an onset critical temperature Tc of ∼4 K. The pressure dependence of Tc is negligible, in contrast to the increasing or dome-shaped Tc(P) reported for isostructural layered diselenide superconductors. High-pressure magnetoresistance and Hall measurements indicate successive modifications of the electronic structure with Fermi surface changes occurring at ~5 GPa and ∼12 GPa.

We present the pressure evolution of charge density wave (CDW) order and the onset of superconductivity (SC) in a 1T-VSe2 single crystal, inferred from resistance and magnetoresistance measurements under high quasihydrostatic pressure. As pressure is raised the CDW is initially strengthened: its ordering temperature (∼100 K at ambient P) shows only a slight increase up to 5 GPa. Beyond this, the CDW-related resistance anomaly grows more rapidly, the characteristic temperature climbing to ∼290 K by 14.2 GPa. With further compression the CDW-associated resistance feature is then strongly suppressed while the sample becomes significantly more metallic (as evidenced by an increased RRR), and superconductivity appears near ∼15 GPa with an onset critical temperature Tc of ∼4 K. The pressure dependence of Tc is essentially negligible, in contrast to the monotonic rise or dome-shaped Tc(P) reported for isostructural layered diselenide superconductors. High-pressure magnetoresistance and Hall data indicate successive electronic-structure changes, consistent with Fermi-surface reconstructions occurring at 5 and ∼12 GPa.

We report the pressure evolution of charge density wave (CDW) order and the emergence of superconductivity (SC) in 1T-VSe2 single crystal by studying resistance and magnetoresistance behavior under high pressure. Under increasing quasihydrostatic pressure the CDW becomes stronger, with its ordering temperature (∼100K at ambient P) rising only slightly up to 5 GPa. At still higher pressures the CDW-like resistance anomaly grows more rapidly, the characteristic temperature reaching ∼290K at 14.2 GPa. On further pressurization the CDW-related resistance anomaly is then strongly suppressed while the material becomes markedly more metallic (as evidenced by an increased RRR), and superconductivity appears at ∼15 GPa with an onset critical temperature Tc ≈ ∼4 K. The Tc shows negligible pressure dependence, in contrast to the increasing or dome-shaped Tc(P) observed in isostructural layered diselenide superconductors. High-pressure magnetoresistance and Hall data point to successive electronic-structure changes, with Fermi-surface modifications occurring at 5 and ∼12 GPa.

We report the pressure evolution of charge density wave (CDW) order and the emergence of superconductivity (SC) in 1T-VSe2 single crystal through measurements of resistance and magnetoresistance under high pressure. With increasing quasihydrostatic pressure the CDW order is strengthened, the ordering temperature (∼100 K at ambient P) rising only slightly up to 5 GPa. Above 5 GPa the CDW-like resistive anomaly shifts more rapidly to higher temperature, reaching a characteristic temperature of ∼290 K at 14.2 GPa. Upon further compression the CDW-related resistance anomaly is then strongly suppressed while metallicity increases sharply (as clearly evidenced by an increased RRR), and superconductivity appears at ∼15 GPa with an onset critical temperature Tc ≈ 4 K. The pressure dependence of Tc is negligible, in contrast to the monotonic increase or dome-shaped behavior observed in isostructural layered diselenide superconductors. High-pressure magnetoresistance and Hall measurements indicate successive electronic-structure changes, with Fermi-surface modifications occurring at 5 and ∼12 GPa.

By measuring resistance and magnetoresistance under high pressure, we examine the pressure-driven evolution of charge density wave (CDW) order and the onset of superconductivity (SC) in 1T-VSe2 single crystals. Under increasing quasihydrostatic pressure the CDW order is strengthened, with its ordering temperature (≈100 K at ambient pressure) rising only slightly up to 5 GPa. Above this pressure the CDW-like resistance anomaly grows more rapidly, its characteristic temperature reaching ≈290 K at 14.2 GPa. With further pressure increase the resistance anomaly associated with CDW order is then drastically suppressed while metallicity increases sharply (as evidenced by an increased RRR), and superconductivity appears near 15 GPa with an onset critical temperature Tc ≈ 4 K. The pressure dependence of Tc is negligible, in contrast to the increasing or dome-shaped behavior seen in isostructural layered diselenide superconductors. High-pressure magnetoresistance and Hall measurements indicate successive electronic-structure changes, with Fermi-surface modifications at 5 and ≈12 GPa.

We report the demonstration of rapid, first-order sideband transitions between a superconducting resonator and a frequency-modulated transmon qubit. The transmon features a substantial asymmetry between its Josephson junctions, producing a linear portion of the energy band in the vicinity of the resonator frequency. The sideband transitions are driven by a magnetic flux signal of a few hundred MHz applied to the qubit. This flux modulation varies the qubit splitting at a frequency tuned near the detuning between the dressed qubit and resonator frequencies, yielding rates up to 85 MHz for exchanging quanta between the qubit and resonator.

We demonstrate rapid, first-order sideband transitions between a superconducting resonator and a frequency-modulated transmon qubit. The transmon is engineered with a substantial asymmetry between its Josephson junctions, producing a linear portion of the energy band in the vicinity of the resonator frequency. Sideband driving is accomplished by applying a magnetic flux signal of a few hundred MHz to the qubit, which modulates the qubit splitting at a frequency close to the detuning between the dressed qubit and resonator frequencies. This protocol yields exchange rates up to 85 MHz for transferring quanta between the qubit and the resonator.

We demonstrate rapid, first-order sideband transitions between a superconducting resonator and a frequency-modulated transmon qubit. The qubit is engineered with a pronounced asymmetry between its Josephson junctions, producing a linear portion of the energy band in the vicinity of the resonator frequency. The sideband transitions are induced by a magnetic flux signal of a few hundred MHz coupled to the qubit, which modulates the qubit splitting at a frequency close to the detuning between the dressed qubit and resonator frequencies and yields exchange rates up to 85 MHz for transferring quanta between the qubit and resonator.

We demonstrate rapid, first-order sideband transitions between a superconducting resonator and a frequency-modulated transmon qubit. A substantial asymmetry between the qubit's Josephson junctions produces a linear portion of the energy band situated near the resonator frequency. The sideband transitions are driven by a magnetic flux signal of a few hundred MHz coupled to the qubit. That flux modulation varies the qubit splitting at a frequency close to the detuning between the dressed qubit and resonator frequencies, yielding exchange rates up to 85 MHz for transferring quanta between the qubit and resonator.

We demonstrate rapid, first-order sideband transitions between a superconducting resonator and a frequency-modulated transmon qubit. The transmon exhibits a substantial asymmetry between its Josephson junctions, producing a linear section of the energy band near the resonator frequency. Sideband transitions are driven by a magnetic flux signal of a few hundred megahertz coupled to the qubit, which modulates the qubit splitting at a frequency close to the detuning between the dressed qubit and resonator frequencies. This produces exchange rates up to 85 MHz for transferring quanta between the qubit and resonator.

We analyze a quantum dot coupled to a topological superconductor and a normal lead, focusing on the interplay between Kondo- and Majorana-induced couplings in such quantum dots. The Majorana-induced coupling arises from the presence of Majorana zero-energy modes localized, for example, at the ends of the one-dimensional superconductor. To map out the system's phase diagram as a function of Kondo and Majorana interactions, we employ a renormalization-group analysis, a slave-boson mean-field theory, and numerical simulations based on the density-matrix renormalization-group method. We find that, beyond the familiar Kondo fixed point, the renormalization-group flow can approach a distinct fixed point governed by the Majorana-induced coupling; this Majorana fixed point is marked by nontrivial correlations between the localized spin on the dot and the fermion parity of the topological superconductor and the normal lead. Finally, we calculate experimentally accessible quantities, including the differential tunneling conductance and the impurity-spin susceptibility, which exhibit characteristic signatures of the Majorana fixed point.

We analyze a quantum dot coupled to a topological superconductor and a normal lead, focusing on the interplay between Kondo-and Majorana-induced couplings in the dot. The Majorana-induced coupling arises from the presence of Majorana zero-energy modes localized, for example, at the ends of a one-dimensional superconductor. We map out the phase diagram as a function of the Kondo and Majorana interaction strengths using a renormalization-group analysis, a slave-boson mean-field theory, and numerical simulations based on the density-matrix renormalization-group method. We demonstrate that, in addition to the familiar Kondo fixed point, the renormalization-group flow can approach a distinct fixed point governed by the Majorana-induced coupling; this Majorana-controlled fixed point is marked by nontrivial correlations between the localized spin on the dot and the fermion parity of the topological superconductor and the normal lead. We compute several experimentally accessible quantities, such as the differential tunneling conductance and the impurity-spin susceptibility, which reveal characteristic features of the Majorana fixed point.

We examine a quantum dot coupled to a topological superconductor and to a normal lead, and we analyze the interplay between Kondo- and Majorana-induced couplings in such quantum-dot systems. The Majorana-induced coupling originates from Majorana zero-energy modes that are localized, for example, at the ends of the one-dimensional topological superconductor. We map out the phase diagram as a function of the Kondo and Majorana interaction strengths using a combination of renormalization-group analysis, a slave-boson mean-field approach, and numerical density-matrix renormalization-group simulations. We find that, besides the familiar Kondo fixed point, the renormalization-group flow can be attracted to a distinct fixed point governed by the Majorana-induced coupling; this Majorana fixed point is marked by nontrivial correlations between the localized spin on the dot and the fermion parity of both the topological superconductor and the normal lead. To characterize experimental signatures of this regime, we calculate measurable quantities including the differential tunneling conductance and the impurity-spin susceptibility, which display peculiar features diagnostic of the Majorana fixed point.

We analyze a quantum dot that is coupled both to a topological superconductor and to a normal lead, focusing on the interplay between Kondo- and Majorana-induced couplings in the dot. The Majorana coupling originates from Majorana zero-energy modes, which may be localized, for instance, at the ends of a one-dimensional superconductor. To map out the phase behavior as the Kondo and Majorana interactions are varied, we employ a renormalization-group analysis, a slave-boson mean-field approach, and density-matrix renormalization-group numerical simulations. Besides the familiar Kondo fixed point, we find that the renormalization flow can be attracted to a distinct fixed point governed by the Majorana-induced coupling; this Majorana fixed point exhibits nontrivial correlations between the dot’s localized spin and the fermion parity of the topological superconductor together with the normal lead. We calculate experimentally accessible observables, including the differential tunneling conductance and the impurity-spin susceptibility, and identify signatures that are distinctive of the Majorana-controlled fixed point.

We analyze a quantum dot hybridized with a topological superconductor and a normal lead, focusing on the competition between Kondo and Majorana-induced couplings in the dot. The Majorana coupling arises from Majorana zero-energy modes localized, for example, at the ends of the one-dimensional superconductor. Using a renormalization-group analysis, a slave-boson mean-field theory, and numerical density-matrix renormalization-group simulations, we map out the phase diagram as a function of the Kondo and Majorana interaction strengths. Besides the familiar Kondo fixed point, we find that the flow can be attracted to a distinct fixed point dominated by the Majorana-induced coupling; this Majorana fixed point is marked by nontrivial correlations between the localized spin on the dot and the fermion parity of the topological superconductor together with the normal lead. We calculate experimentally accessible observables, including the differential tunneling conductance and the impurity-spin susceptibility, and identify signatures that are distinctive of the Majorana-controlled fixed point.

Correlations between critical temperature, the modulation vector q, and oxygen non-stoichiometry were examined for three series of compositions of the 2212 bismuth cuprates. For this study three samples were prepared under different conditions: the pure phase Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8 (synthesized in argon), Bi2.1Sr1.9CaCu2O8+δ (synthesized in air), and the mixture Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (synthesized in air). Oxygen intercalation and deintercalation were performed, and the q vector and critical temperature were measured. In every series the q vector decreases as the oxygen content δ increases. Comparison among the series further indicates that the Bi/Sr ratio affects q. It is also demonstrated that, while oxygen non-stoichiometry can dramatically influence the Tcs, it is not the only factor: the cationic composition also plays a role. The importance of phase purity for the physical interpretation is discussed, taking into account that the air-synthesized “Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ” composition is not single-phase and may correspond to various members of the non-stoichiometric phase Bi2+xSr2−xCaCu2O8+δ.

We investigated the correlations between critical temperature, modulation vector q and oxygen non-stoichiometry for three series of compositions belonging to the 2212 bismuth cuprates. For this exploration three samples were prepared in different conditions: the pure phase Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8 (synthesized in argon), Bi2.1Sr1.9CaCu2O8+δ (synthesized in air), and the mixture Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (synthesized in air). Oxygen intercalation and disintercalation were carried out and both the q vector and the critical temperature were measured. In each series of oxides, the q vector decreases as the oxygen content δ increases. Comparison of the different series also shows that the Bi/Sr ratio affects q. It is also demonstrated that, while oxygen non-stoichiometry can dramatically influence Tcs, it is not the only factor, with the cationic composition also playing a role. The importance of phase purity for the physical interpretation is discussed, taking into account that the air-synthesized “Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ” composition is not a single phase and may correspond to various members of the non-stoichiometric phase Bi2+xSr2−xCaCu2O8+δ.

We investigated the correlations among critical temperature, the structural modulation vector q, and oxygen non-stoichiometry for three series of compositions within the 2212 bismuth cuprates. Three sample sets were prepared under different conditions: the phase-pure Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8 synthesized in argon, Bi2.1Sr1.9CaCu2O8+δ synthesized in air, and the mixed-composition Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ synthesized in air. Oxygen intercalation and deintercalation treatments were applied, and both the q vector and critical temperature were measured. For every series, q was found to decrease as the oxygen content δ increased. A comparison between series further indicates that the Bi/Sr ratio has a measurable effect on q. It is also shown that, although oxygen non-stoichiometry can strongly affect critical temperatures (Tcs), it is not the sole determining factor: the cationic composition contributes as well. Finally, we discuss the importance of phase purity for physical interpretation, noting that the air-synthesized “Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ” composition is not a single phase and may represent various members of the non-stoichiometric family Bi2+xSr2−xCaCu2O8+δ.

REBCO (RE = rare earth) based high temperature superconducting (HTS) wires are increasingly employed in the development of electric and electromagnetic devices for a range of industrial, scientific, and medical applications. In recent years, efforts to deploy second generation (2G) HTS wires for many of these applications have driven the need for further increases in engineering current density (J e). This demand is particularly strong for high magnetic field applications, where the higher J e of a REBCO wire—combined with its larger irreversibility fields and greater mechanical strength—already provides a substantial advantage over other superconducting wires. Reducing the overall thickness of the wire is an effective route to raise J e, making the use of a thinner substrate an attractive strategy. Using our IBAD-MOCVD (ion beam assisted deposition-metal organic chemical vapor deposition) technology, we have successfully fabricated 2G HTS wires on Hastelloy® C276 substrates only 30 μm thick. Replacing the typical 50 μm substrate with this 30 μm substrate, while maintaining the same critical current (I c), increases J e by 30% to 45%, depending on the copper stabilizer thickness. Here we present the fabrication and characterization of 2G HTS wires made on the 30 μm Hastelloy® C276 substrate. With optimization of the processing protocol, the surface of the thinner Hastelloy® C276 substrate can be electropolished to the quality required for buffer-stack deposition. Using the same architecture as on the standard 50 μm substrate, the buffer stack on the 30 μm substrate exhibited an in-plane texture with a Δ of around 6.7° in the LaMnO3 cap layer. Low-temperature in-field transport measurements indicate that wires on the thinner substrate achieve equivalent superconducting performance—most importantly the I c—compared to those on the 50 μm substrate. It is expected that 2G HTS wires fabricated on the 30 μm thick Hastelloy® C276 substrate, the thinnest and with the highest J e to date, will greatly benefit applications such as high-field magnets and high-current cables.

REBCO (RE = rare earth) based high temperature superconducting (HTS) wires are increasingly being deployed in the development of electric and electromagnetic devices across industrial, scientific and medical fields. Recent efforts to employ second generation (2G) HTS wires for many of these applications have driven the need for further increases in their engineering current density (J e). This need is particularly acute for high magnetic field applications, where a higher J e, together with elevated irreversibility fields and superior mechanical strength, already makes REBCO wires advantageous over other superconductors. A straightforward and effective route to raise J e is to reduce the total thickness of the wire, making a thinner substrate an attractive solution. Using our IBAD-MOCVD (ion beam assisted deposition–metal organic chemical vapor deposition) technology, we have successfully fabricated 2G HTS wires on a Hastelloy® C276 substrate reduced to 30 μm thickness. Replacing the typical 50 μm substrate with this 30 μm substrate, while maintaining the same critical current (I c), yields a J e increase of 30% to 45% depending on the copper stabilizer thickness. Here we report the fabrication and characterization of 2G HTS wires deposited on the 30 μm Hastelloy® C276 substrate. With optimization of the processing protocol, the surface of the thinner Hastelloy® C276 substrate can be electropolished to the quality required for buffer stack deposition. Using the same architecture as on the standard 50 μm substrate, the buffer stack on the 30 μm substrate exhibited an in-plane texture with a Δ of around 6.7° in the LaMnO3 cap layer. Low-temperature, in-field transport measurements indicate that wires on the thinner substrate achieve equivalent superconducting performance—most importantly the I c—compared to those on the 50 μm substrate. It is expected that 2G HTS wires produced on the 30 μm Hastelloy® C276 substrate, the thinnest and with the highest J e to date, will substantially benefit applications such as high field magnets and high current cables.

REBCO (RE = rare earth) based high temperature superconducting (HTS) wires are increasingly deployed in electric and electromagnetic devices across industrial, scientific and medical fields. Recent efforts to employ second generation (2G) HTS wires in high magnetic field applications demand further increases in engineering current density (Je); in such applications the higher Je of REBCO conductors, together with their larger irreversibility fields and superior mechanical strength, already provides a substantial advantage over alternative superconducting wires. Reducing overall wire thickness is an effective route to raise Je, making the use of a thinner substrate an attractive strategy. Using our IBAD-MOCVD (ion beam assisted deposition–metal organic chemical vapor deposition) process, we have fabricated 2G HTS wires on Hastelloy® C276 substrates only 30 μm thick. Replacing the conventional 50 μm substrate by this 30 μm substrate yields a Je increase of 30% to 45% for the same critical current (Ic), the exact gain depending on the copper stabilizer thickness. Here we report the fabrication and characterization of these 2G wires on 30 μm Hastelloy® C276: with optimized processing the thinner substrate surface can be electropolished to the quality required for buffer stack deposition. With the same architecture as used on the standard 50 μm substrate, the buffer stack on the 30 μm material exhibits an in-plane texture with a Δ of approximately 6.7° in the LaMnO3 cap layer. Low-temperature in-field transport measurements indicate that the wires on the thinner substrate achieve superconducting performance—most critically Ic—equivalent to those on the 50 μm substrate. The 2G HTS wires produced on 30 μm Hastelloy® C276, the thinnest and exhibiting the highest Je reported to date, are expected to greatly benefit applications such as high field magnets and high current cables.

REBCO (RE = rare earth) based high temperature superconducting (HTS) wires are increasingly being deployed in the development of electric and electromagnetic devices for a range of industrial, scientific, and medical applications. Over the past several years, efforts to employ second generation (2G) HTS wires in many of these applications have driven the need to further raise their engineering current density (J e). This demand is particularly acute for high magnetic field applications, where the larger J e of REBCO wires—alongside their higher irreversibility fields and superior mechanical strength—already provides a significant advantage over other superconducting wires. Reducing the overall tape thickness is an effective route to increase J e, making the adoption of a thinner substrate an obvious and attractive strategy. Using our IBAD-MOCVD (ion beam assisted deposition–metal organic chemical vapor deposition) technology, we have produced 2G HTS wires on a Hastelloy® C276 substrate only 30 μm thick. Replacing the typical 50 μm substrate with this thinner 30 μm substrate, while maintaining the same critical current (I c), yields a J e increase of 30% to 45%, depending on the copper stabilizer thickness. In this paper we report the fabrication and characterization of these 2G HTS wires manufactured on 30 μm Hastelloy® C276. We demonstrate that, with optimized processing, the surface of the thinner Hastelloy® C276 can be electropolished to the quality required for buffer stack deposition. Using the same architecture as on the standard 50 μm substrate, the buffer stack on the 30 μm substrate exhibited an in-plane texture with a Δ of around 6.7° in the LaMnO3 cap layer. Low-temperature, in-field transport measurements indicate that wires on the thinner substrate achieve superconducting performance equivalent to those on the 50 μm substrate, most importantly matching I c. The 2G HTS wires fabricated on the 30 μm Hastelloy® C276 substrate—the thinnest and with the highest J e reported to date—are expected to substantially benefit applications such as high field magnets and high current cables.

REBCO (RE = rare earth) based high temperature superconducting (HTS) wires are now being utilized for the development of electric and electromagnetic devices for various industrial, scientific and medical applications. Over the past several years, growing efforts to deploy second generation (2G) HTS wires in these applications have driven a need to further raise their engineering current density (J e). This requirement is particularly pressing for applications involving high magnetic fields, where a REBCO wire’s higher J e, together with its larger irreversibility fields and greater mechanical strength, already provides a substantial advantage over other superconducting wires. Reducing the overall thickness of the wire is an effective route to increase J e, making the use of a thinner substrate an obvious and attractive strategy. Employing our IBAD-MOCVD (ion beam assisted deposition–metal organic chemical vapor deposition) technology, we have successfully fabricated 2G HTS wires on Hastelloy® C276 substrates only 30 μm thick. Replacing the typical 50 μm substrate with this thinner 30 μm substrate, while maintaining the same critical current (I c), yields an increase in J e of 30% to 45%, depending on the copper stabilizer thickness. In this paper, we report the fabrication and characterization of these 2G HTS wires produced on the 30 μm thick Hastelloy® C276 substrate. With optimization of the processing protocol, the surface of the thinner Hastelloy® C276 substrate can be readily electropolished to the quality required for deposition of the buffer stack. Using the same architecture as on the standard 50 μm substrate, the buffer stack deposited on the 30 μm substrate exhibited an in-plane texture with a Δ of around 6.7° in the LaMnO3 cap layer. Low-temperature in-field transport measurements indicate that the wires on the thinner substrate achieved superconducting performance equivalent—most importantly in I c—to those on the 50 μm thick substrate. It is expected the 2G HTS wires made on the 30 μm thick Hastelloy® C276 substrate, the thinnest and with the highest J e to date, will greatly benefit such applications as high field magnets and high current cables.

New tunneling measurements in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ reveal quasiparticle excitation gaps, Δ, reaching up to 60 meV in underdoped crystals with Tc=70 K. These gap values are nearly 3 times larger than those observed in overdoped crystals with comparable Tc. Despite this pronounced difference in gap magnitude, the tunneling spectra show qualitatively similar features across the entire doping range. Detailed analysis of the spectra, including the Josephson IcRn product measured in break junctions, indicates that these energy gaps are predominantly of superconducting origin.

We report new tunneling measurements on Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ that show quasiparticle excitation gaps, Δ, reaching up to 60 meV in underdoped crystals with Tc = 70 K. These gap values are nearly three times larger than those found in overdoped crystals with similar Tc. Despite the substantial differences in gap magnitude, the tunneling spectra retain qualitatively similar characteristics across the entire doping range. Detailed analysis of the spectra, including the Josephson IcRn product measured in break junctions, indicates that these energy gaps are predominantly of superconducting origin.

We present new tunneling measurements on Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ that reveal quasiparticle excitation gaps, Δ, as large as 60 meV in underdoped crystals with Tc = 70 K. These gap magnitudes are almost three times those observed in overdoped crystals with comparable Tc. Even with such substantial differences in Δ, the tunneling spectra retain qualitatively similar characteristics throughout the entire doping range. A detailed analysis of the spectra, including the Josephson IcRn product measured in break junctions, indicates that these energy gaps are predominantly of superconducting origin.

Approaches based on open quantum systems are extensively employed to describe phenomena in physics, chemistry and biology. A paradigmatic case is the electronic excitation transfer that takes place in the initial stage of photosynthesis, where the harvested energy is delivered with remarkably high efficiency to a reaction center. This transport is influenced by the dynamics of a structured vibrational environment, which renders classical-computer simulations extremely challenging. Here we propose an analog quantum simulator for complex open-system dynamics that incorporates a precisely engineered quantum environment. The proposed implementation uses superconducting circuits, a well-established technology. As an explicit example, we show that exciton transport in the Fenna–Matthews–Olson photosynthetic complex can be simulated feasibly. Our scheme enables a controllable single-molecule simulation and the study of energy transfer pathways as well as non-Markovian noise-correlation effects.

Open quantum system approaches are widely used to describe physical, chemical, and biological systems. A notable example is the electronic excitation transfer occurring in the initial stage of photosynthesis, where harvested energy is conveyed with remarkably high efficiency to a reaction center. This transport is influenced by the motion of a structured vibrational environment, which renders simulations on a classical computer very demanding. Here we propose an analog quantum simulator of complex open system dynamics that incorporates a precisely engineered quantum environment. Our implementation is based on superconducting circuits, a well-established technology. As an example, we demonstrate that it is feasible to simulate exciton transport in the Fenna–Matthews–Olson photosynthetic complex. The approach permits controllable single-molecule simulation and the investigation of energy transfer pathways as well as non-Markovian noise-correlation effects.

Open quantum system methods are extensively employed to describe phenomena in physics, chemistry, and biology. A well-known case is the electronic excitation transfer occurring in the initial stage of photosynthesis, where absorbed energy is delivered with remarkably high efficiency to a reaction center. This transport is influenced by the dynamics of a structured vibrational environment, making classical computer simulations extremely challenging. Here we propose an analog quantum simulator for complex open-system dynamics that incorporates a precisely engineered quantum environment. The experimental design is based on superconducting circuits, a mature and well-established technology. Our scheme enables controllable single-molecule simulations and permits the study of energy transfer pathways as well as non-Markovian noise-correlation effects.

We study 2D surface superconductivity in large magnetic fields applied parallel to the surface. We show that spin-orbit interaction at the surface modifies the behavior of the inhomogeneous Larkin-Ovchinnikov-Fulde-Ferrell (LOFF) superconducting state that appears above the fields determined by the paramagnetic criterion. Strong surface spin-orbit coupling markedly extends the parameter range over which the LOFF phase exists; this phase manifests as periodic superconducting stripes running along the direction of the field on the surface and produces anisotropic properties. Our results provide a tool for studying surface superconductivity as a function of doping.

We study 2D surface superconductivity in high magnetic fields applied parallel to the surface. Recent experiments on twisted bilayer graphene have revealed a high-temperature parent state hosting massless Dirac fermions and exhibiting broken electronic flavor symmetry; from this parent state both superconductivity and correlated insulators develop at lower temperatures. We propose that the superconducting and correlated insulating orders are connected by Wess-Zumino-Witten terms, so that defects of one order carry quanta of the other and skyrmion fluctuations of the correlated insulator provide a "mechanism" for superconductivity. We provide a comprehensive listing of plausible low-temperature orders together with the parent flavor symmetry-breaking orders. The previously characterized topological nature of the band structure of twisted bilayer graphene plays an important role in this analysis.

The critical field of a thin superconducting film with a blind circular hole is determined theoretically. It is shown that the value of the critical field is sensitive to the bottom thickness, whereas the orbital momentum, i.e., the number of vortices which nucleate inside the hole, is not sensitive. A simple boundary condition for a steplike thin film is derived and employed for comparative numerical analysis of the superconductivity nucleation in a microdisk and near the hole. By increasing the thickness of the bottom of a blind hole one can transform the hole into a disk of the same radius that rests on top of the film. We show that such a transformation produces a jump in the number of vortices which nucleate at the critical magnetic field inside the perimeter of the hole (the disk). We also report the results of Bitter decoration experiments on a thin superconducting film patterned with a lattice of open or blind holes. It is found (in accordance with the calculation) that the bottom thickness has only a weak influence on the number of vortices captured by a hole during cooling of the sample in a constant perpendicular magnetic field. All the experimental results are explained under the assumption that vortices nucleated inside a hole remain there during the cooling process and that no additional vortices enter the hole. © 1996 The American Physical Society.

We determine theoretically the critical field of a thin superconducting film containing a blind circular hole. The analysis demonstrates that the critical-field magnitude is strongly affected by the thickness of the hole bottom, whereas the orbital momentum, i.e., the number of vortices that nucleate within the hole, remains essentially insensitive to that thickness. A simple boundary condition appropriate for a steplike thin film is derived and employed in a comparative numerical study of superconductivity nucleation in a microdisk and in the region near the hole. Increasing the bottom thickness of a blind hole until it becomes a disk of the same radius resting on the film is shown to produce a discontinuous change in the number of vortices nucleating at the critical magnetic field inside the hole’s (disk’s) perimeter. We also present Bitter-decoration experiments on a thin superconducting film patterned with a lattice of open or blind holes. Consistent with the calculations, the experiments show that the bottom thickness exerts only a weak effect on how many vortices a hole captures during cooling at fixed perpendicular magnetic field. All experimental observations are accounted for by assuming that vortices nucleated inside a hole remain trapped there during cooling and that no additional vortices enter the hole. © 1996 The American Physical Society.

The boson theory is reformulated into a version suitable for describing the mixed state in the rare-earth compounds RRh4B4, RxMo6S8, and RxMo6Se6. We self-consistently calculate the magnetic field produced by the persistent vortex currents together with the spin magnetization induced by that field. A nonuniform spin magnetization, sharing the periodicity of the flux-line lattice, appears in the crystals. The total flux, given by the sum of the spin and current contributions, is quantized. This quantization produces current inversion and thereby an attractive interaction between vortices. The computed magnetization shows a tendency toward type-I or type II-1 superconducting behavior even for large κ. The strong suppression of Hc2 near the Curie or Néel temperature accounts for the experimental observations on DyxMo6S8 and ErxMo6S8.

The boson theory is recast in a form suitable for describing the mixed state of the rare-earth compounds RRh4B4, RxMo6S8, and RxMo6Se6. We self-consistently compute the magnetic field produced by the persistent vortex currents together with the spin magnetization induced by that field. A nonuniform spin magnetization emerges in the crystals, exhibiting the periodicity of the flux-line lattice. The total flux, given by the sum of the spin and current contributions, is quantized. This flux quantization brings about current inversion and thereby an attractive interaction between vortices. The resulting magnetization shows a tendency toward type-I or type II-1 superconducting behavior even at large κ. The pronounced suppression of Hc2 near the Curie or Nèel temperature accounts for the experimental observations in DyxMo6S8 and ErxMo6S8.

In a superconductor, where electrons are paired, the density of unpaired electrons is expected to vanish as temperature approaches zero. Consequently, radiation detectors based on breaking of pairs promise supreme sensitivity, which we demonstrate using an aluminium superconducting microwave resonator. We show that the resonator also permits study of the response of the superconductor’s electron system to pair-breaking photons, to microwave photons, and to changes in temperature. By carefully filtering the radiation from a blackbody source, a large range of radiation power at 1.54 THz can be selected. We identify two regimes. At high radiation power, fluctuations in the electron system due to the random arrival rate of photons are resolved, yielding a straightforward measure of the optical efficiency (48±8%) and demonstrating an unprecedented detector sensitivity. At low radiation power, fluctuations are dominated by excess quasiparticles, the number of which is determined from their recombination lifetime.

A Weyl semimetal is a novel state of matter that hosts Weyl fermions as emergent quasiparticles. These Weyl fermions arise at isolated points of bulk band degeneracy—Weyl nodes—which are connected solely via the crystal’s boundary by exotic Fermi arcs. The length of the Fermi arc serves as a measure of the topological strength, since the only way to destroy the Weyl nodes is to annihilate them in pairs in the reciprocal space. To date, Weyl semimetals have been realized only in the TaAs class. Here we propose a tunable Weyl state in MoxW1−xTe2, in which Weyl nodes appear as touching points between metallic pockets. We show that the Fermi arc length can be varied as a function of Mo concentration, thereby tuning the topological strength. Our results offer an experimentally feasible route to realizing Weyl physics in the layered compound MoxW1−xTe2, where non-saturating magneto-resistance and pressure-driven superconductivity have been observed.

Using Ru NMR, we examined and compared the magnetic properties of three-dimensional (3D) perovskites SrRuO3 (ferromagnet) and CaRuO3 (exchange enhanced paramagnet) with those of the two-dimensional (2D) layered perovskite Sr2RuO4 (superconductor), RuO2, and Ru metal (Pauli paramagnet). We observe that 4d-spin contributions dominate both the Knight shift and the 101(1/T1) of Ru in SrRuO3, CaRuO3, and Sr2RuO4, whereas such contributions are not dominant in nonmagnetic RuO2 and in Ru metal. The experimental findings — specifically that the Stoner factor for CaRuO3 is close to 1 and that the correlation factor inferred from the modified-Korringa behavior of Ru-T1 is much less than 1 — imply that CaRuO3 is a nearly ferromagnetic (FM) metal whose spin fluctuations (SF) are dominated by low-frequency, long-wavelength components. Although the Stoner factor for Sr2RuO4 likewise signals proximity to ferromagnetism, the character of the SF in Sr2RuO4 differs from that in CaRuO3. When combined with prior 17O NMR results on Sr2RuO4, our data indicate that the in-plane low-frequency components of SF are exchange enhanced with little q dependence owing to the 2D electronic structure (i.e., 2D nearly FM), while the out-of-plane low-frequency component reveals antiferromagnetic (AFM) SF between layers at low temperature. We propose that the evolution of SF from 3D to 2D nearly FM behavior is relevant to the emergence of spin-triplet superconductivity in Sr2RuO4. In particular, the 2D nearly FM SF in the in-plane 4dxy–pπ band may play an important role in stabilizing a parallel-spin pairing state within the basal plane among the possible spin-triplet order-parameter representations in Sr2RuO4.

Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials hold the potential to transform numerous fields by offering materials with advanced functional applications. Examples of these 2D nanomaterials include graphene and its derivative, graphene nanosheets, graphene nanoplatelets, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), transition metal dichalcogenides, MXenes, covalent organic frameworks (COFs), hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), etc., which are employed in energy storage devices, hybrid capacitors, supercapacitors, photovoltaic cells, sensors, photoluminescent devices, semiconductors, diodes, transistors, room temperature superconductor, anti-corrosive coating, insulating materials, etc. In this review article we concentrate on various synthetic approaches, design strategies, and processing methods for 2D nanomaterials. We discuss different synthesis practices such as ultrasonic exfoliation, Hummers, chemical vapor deposition, hydrothermal/solvothermal, micromechanical exfoliation, ligand-assisted approach, small molecule mediated synthesis, etc. Controlling the processing route is critical for tailoring the properties of 2D nanomaterials, since those properties strongly depend on factors like size-to-volume ratio, exfoliation technique, thickness and lateral dimensions of the 2D sheets, porous versus nonporous character, and the percentage of organic and inorganic content, etc. Emphasis is placed on how surface properties influence the physical, mechanical, optical, and electrical behaviors and consequent applications of 2D nanomaterials, and a few important applications are also discussed. This review adopts a holistic perspective by incorporating social, economic, and ecological aspects of 2D nanomaterial synthesis and aims to assist in the design, fabrication, and application-specific development of 2D nanomaterials for various nanodevices.

Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials possess the potential to transform technologies by offering materials with advanced functional applications. These 2D nanomaterials — i.e., graphene and its derivative, graphene nanosheets, graphene nanoplatelets, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), transition metal dichalcogenides, MXenes, covalent organic frameworks (COFs), hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), etc. — are employed in energy storage devices, hybrid capacitors, supercapacitors, photovoltaic cells, sensors, photoluminescent devices, semiconductors, diodes, transistors, room temperature superconductor, anti-corrosive coating, insulating materials, and other applications. In this review article, we focus on the various synthetic approaches, design strategies, and processing methods for 2D nanomaterials. Synthesis practices such as ultrasonic exfoliation, Hummers, chemical vapor deposition, hydrothermal/solvothermal techniques, micromechanical exfoliation, ligand-assisted approaches, and small molecule mediated synthesis, among others, are discussed. Controlling the processing route is crucial for tailoring desired properties because nanomaterial behavior depends strongly on factors like size-to-volume ratio, exfoliation technique, sheet thickness and dimensions, porous versus nonporous character, and the fraction of organic versus inorganic content. Emphasis is placed on how surface properties influence the physical, mechanical, optical, and electrical characteristics and applications of 2D nanomaterials, and several key applications are examined. This review adopts a holistic perspective by incorporating social, economic, and ecological considerations in the synthesis of 2D nanomaterials and aims to aid the design, fabrication, and application-specific development of 2D nanomaterials for diverse nanodevices.

CONSPECTUS: Achieving phase changes by selectively exciting particular vibrational modes with light in molecular and condensed-phase systems has been a longstanding objective of laser science. To date, however, control over phases has predominantly relied on coherent fields produced by femtosecond pulsed lasers at near-infrared or visible wavelengths. Recent advances in generating intense femtosecond pulses in the mid-infrared—tunable into resonance with infrared-active crystal lattice modes—are now pushing the field forward. Mode-selective vibrational excitation is especially compelling in complex oxides with strong electronic correlations, where even minute distortions of the crystal lattice can produce colossal alterations of electronic and magnetic properties. In this Account, we review recent work aimed at steering the collective phase state of solids via selective excitation of lattice modes. The central element of the underlying physics is the nonlinear coupling of a resonantly driven phonon to other (Raman-active) modes arising from lattice anharmonicities, a mechanism theoretically framed as ionic Raman scattering in the 1970s. Such nonlinear phononic processes produce a rectification of the directly excited infrared-active mode and generate a net displacement of the crystal along the coordinates of all anharmonically coupled modes. We describe both the theoretical framework and experimental demonstrations of this effect, employing femtosecond optical spectroscopy and ultrafast X-ray diffraction at a free electron laser. The nonlinear lattice dynamics observed are shown to trigger electronic and magnetic phase transitions in a variety of complex oxides, including insulator–metal transitions, melting of charge/orbital order, and magnetic switching in manganites. In addition, selective vibrational excitation can drive high-TC cuprates into a transient structural state with enhanced superconductivity. Merging nonlinear phononics with ultrafast crystallography at X-ray free electron lasers may offer new design principles for creating materials that display these exotic behaviors in equilibrium.

C-axis-oriented HgBa2Ca2Cu3O8+δ thin films exhibit superconductivity above 130 K. At 5 K the superconducting critical current density (Jc) reaches 23 MA/cm2 in the absence of a magnetic field and remains above 2 MA/cm2 in a 5 T magnetic field with the field along the c axis. A zero-field Jc of 0.5 MA/cm2 is preserved to temperatures exceeding 100 K, indicating that the mercury-based cuprate thin films are promising for applications in superconducting electronic devices operating above 100 K.

At 5 K, the superconducting critical current density (Jc) of the film is 23 MA/cm2 in the absence of a magnetic field and remains above 2 MA/cm2 in a 5 T magnetic field applied along the c axis. A zero-field Jc of 0.5 MA/cm2 is sustained to temperatures exceeding 100 K, indicating that the mercury-based cuprate thin films are promising for superconducting electronic devices operating above 100 K.

High temperature superconducting (HTS) flux pumps can produce ultra-high currents (>1 kA) without the need for thermally inefficient room temperature current leads. Such ultra-high currents permit physically smaller magnets with substantially lower inductance, thereby unlocking new design opportunities. However, limited by intrinsically low electrical power efficiencies, existing HTS flux pumps cannot deliver high voltage or high power. In this work, we design, devise, and experimentally verify a transformer–rectifier type HTS flux pump based on Jc(B) switching. We demonstrate that rectification can be realized by exploiting the HTS E–J relation under applied DC magnetic fields. A quasi-persistent current of 54.5 A has been achieved at 77 K, the magnitude being limited solely by the load coil critical current. Moreover, we derive the electrical power efficiencies for both half-wave and full-wave flux pump configurations, and we show that the fundamental Jc(B) mechanism affords significantly higher efficiency than existing HTS flux pumps. This advancement overturns the common view that HTS flux pumps are useful only for maintaining, rather than rapidly ramping, magnetic fields.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG) coated magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles with diameters of 12 nm, 15 nm, and 16 nm were synthesized by the conventional co-precipitation method. The samples’ structure and morphology were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). AC magnetic susceptibility measurements were performed using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), while DC magnetic measurements were carried out on a commercial Quantum Design superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID). XRD patterns indicated the sole presence of the inverse cubic spinel phase of Fe3O4 in all samples. Histograms derived from the TEM images show narrow size distributions, with average sizes closely matching those obtained from the XRD data via Scherrer’s formula. The temperature dependence of both coercivity and saturation magnetization, determined from magnetic hysteresis loops, exhibited considerable deviations from Bloch’s and Kneller’s laws. The size dependence of coercivity and saturation magnetization was non-monotonic at nearly all temperatures. These observations are discussed and attributed primarily to finite size effects, together with the presence of inter-particle interactions and spin-glass structures arising from frozen, canted surface spins at low temperatures.

Introducing oxygen into the chain-like bismuth sulfide Bi2S3 yields two notable functional compounds—photoelectric Bi2O2S and superconducting Bi4O4S3—both featuring PbO-like [Bi2O2] layers. Nanoscale Bi2O2S crystals were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method and exhibit an indirect band gap of 1.12 eV. This semiconductor displays an excellent photoelectric response when irradiated with a visible-light lamp at room temperature. Both theoretical calculations and a packing-factor model indicate that the loosely packed Bi2O2S structure is an excellent photoelectric material. Annealing the Bi2O2S phase at 500 °C in an evacuated quartz tube produced nanocrystals of Bi4O4S3. Powder X-ray diffraction and electron microscope analyses (SEM, TEM, EDX) confirmed the thermal decomposition of orthorhombic Bi2O2S to tetragonal Bi4O4S3. Temperature-dependent measurements of electrical resistivity and magnetic susceptibility reveal a superconducting transition temperature of 4.6 K for Bi4O4S3. These results also provide a new method using thermal decomposition to prepare a new phase without high-temperature reaction.

By introducing oxygen into the chain-like bismuth sulfide Bi2S3, two notable functional compounds containing PbO-like [Bi2O2] layers emerge: Bi2O2S (photoelectric) and Bi4O4S3 (superconducting). This semiconductor exhibits an excellent photoelectric response when irradiated by a visible light lamp at room temperature. Both theoretical calculations and a packing factor model indicate that the loosely packed Bi2O2S structure is an outstanding photoelectric material. Upon annealing the Bi2O2S phase at 500 °C in an evacuated quartz tube, nanocrystals of Bi4O4S3 were produced. Powder X-ray diffraction and electron microscope analyses (SEM, TEM, EDX) confirm a thermal decomposition transition from orthorhombic Bi2O2S to tetragonal Bi4O4S3. The superconducting transition temperature of Bi4O4S3 was determined to be 4.6 K from temperature-dependent measurements of electrical resistivity and magnetic susceptibility. These results also demonstrate a new route—utilizing thermal decomposition—to obtain a new phase without requiring a high-temperature reaction.

The violation of Bell inequalities using two entangled and spatially separated quantum two-level systems (TLSs) is frequently regarded as the clearest demonstration that nature does not conform to local realism. From different but related premises called macrorealism—which macroscopic systems are plausibly expected to satisfy—Leggett and Garg formulated an analogous inequality for a single degree of freedom undergoing coherent oscillations and being probed at successive times. In this work we test that ‘Bell’s inequality in time,’ which a quantum TLS is predicted to violate. Our TLS is implemented as a superconducting quantum circuit in which Rabi oscillations are driven continuously while the system is continuously and weakly measured. The temporal correlations observed in the detector output match quantum-mechanical predictions and violate the Leggett–Garg inequality by five standard deviations.

Violations of Bell inequalities observed with two entangled, spatially separated quantum two-level systems (TLSs) are widely regarded as the most striking evidence that nature does not obey local realism. Under assumptions distinct but related to macrorealism—which macroscopic systems plausibly satisfy—Leggett and Garg derived an analogous inequality for a single degree of freedom undergoing coherent oscillations and being measured at successive times. Here we test that ‘Bell’s inequality in time’, which a quantum TLS is expected to violate. The TLS we employ is a superconducting quantum circuit in which Rabi oscillations are driven continuously while the system is subjected to a continuous weak measurement. Time correlations extracted from the detector output agree with quantum-mechanical predictions and yield a violation of the Leggett–Garg inequality by five standard deviations.

Single-crystal 13C NMR studies were carried out on the quasi-two-dimensional, deuterated kappa-(BEDT-TTF)2Cu[N(CN)(2)]Br system, which lies right at the border of the Mott transition. At low temperature the NMR spectra separate into two components originating from phase-separated metallic (superconducting) and insulating regions. Analysis of these distinct spectra indicates that the Mott transition in this compound is a first-order transition between a pseudogapped superconductor and the simplest commensurate antiferromagnet with a moment of 0.26 mu(B)/dimer.

We investigate the local atomic structure of the new nonmagnetic superconducting system LaO1−xFxBiS2 using neutron diffraction and pair density function analysis. Our data provide evidence for local charge fluctuations tied to a charge disproportionation of the Bi ions in the distorted lattice of superconducting LaO1−xFxBiS2. In-plane, short-range displacements of sulfur atoms up to 0.3 Å break site symmetry and generate two distinct environments around Bi. An out-of-plane motion of the apical sulfur moves it closer to the La-O/F doping layer with increasing x, which may form a charge transfer conduit between the doping layers and the superconducting BiS2 planes. The mechanism for superconductivity may arise from the interplay between charge density fluctuations and an enhanced spin-orbit coupling, suggested theoretically, that induces spin polarization.

Conversion-electron Mössbauer spectroscopy together with superconducting quantum interference device magnetometry were employed to determine, as a function of temperature, both the magnetic hyperfine field and the spontaneous magnetization of ultrathin Fe(110) films grown epitaxially on Ag(111) substrate films. For the first time, hyperfine-field measurements with monolayer resolution and magnetization measurements have been carried out on the same sample, which consists of four Fe(110) atomic layers. A comparison of these results with earlier experiments and theoretical work leads to the following conclusions: (1) the ground-state hyperfine field at the Fe(110)/Ag(111) interface is enhanced relative to bulk, in agreement with other experimental reports but in contradiction to recent band-structure calculations; this discrepancy can be accounted for by including dipolar field contributions at the interface; (2) the relative spin deviation from saturation (T=0) inferred from the hyperfine field matches that obtained from the spontaneous magnetization for both the interface atoms and the center of the film; (3) the spin deviation, ΔM(T), in every layer of the ultrathin Fe film follows a T3/2 law, and therefore a linear M-T relation is not a signature of a two-dimensional ferromagnet as is often stated in the literature; (4) ultrathin Fe films exhibit strong surface and size effects in the thermally excited spin deviation, and currently no theory exists that simultaneously accounts for layer-dependent ground-state moments together with surface and bulk spin waves; and (5) the spin-wave parameter, B, varies between different experiments on similar film systems, suggesting that the real-film interface structure and roughness play an important role.

For the first time, hyperfine-field measurements with monolayer resolution and magnetization measurements have been obtained from the same sample, which consists of four Fe(110) atomic layers. Comparison of the present data with earlier experiments and theoretical results yields the following conclusions: (1) the ground-state hyperfine field at the Fe(110)/Ag(111) interface is enhanced relative to the bulk, consistent with other experiments but in conflict with recent band calculations; this discrepancy can be accounted for by including dipolar field contributions at the interface; (2) the relative spin deviation from saturation (T=0) inferred from the hyperfine field coincides with that determined from the spontaneous magnetization for both the interface atoms and the film center; (3) the spin deviation, ΔM(T), in every layer of the ultrathin Fe film is well described by a T3/2 law; therefore a linear M-T relation is not an intrinsic signature of a two-dimensional ferromagnet, contrary to frequent statements in the literature; (4) ultrathin Fe films exhibit pronounced surface and size effects in the thermally excited spin deviation, and at present no theory incorporates layer-dependent ground-state moments together with surface and bulk spin waves; and (5) the spin-wave parameter, B, varies between different experiments even for comparable film systems, suggesting that the real-film interface structure and roughness play an important role.

Presented is an improved automatic generation control (AGC) system that applies self-tuning adaptive control to both the main AGC loop and the superconducting magnetic energy storage system (SMES). Simulations on a two-area interconnected power system demonstrated that the proposed adaptive control approach very effectively damps oscillations caused by load disturbances, and that its performance is quite insensitive to controller gain parameter changes of the SMES system. A performance evaluation comparing control schemes using adaptive and nonadaptive controllers in the main AGC and in the SMES control loops is presented. The performance improvement afforded by the adaptive scheme was particularly pronounced for load changes of random magnitude and duration.

An improved automatic generation control (AGC) system is described that uses self-tuning adaptive control in both the main AGC loop and the superconducting magnetic energy storage (SMES) system. Computer simulations on a two-area interconnected power system showed the proposed adaptive control scheme to be highly effective at damping oscillations caused by load disturbances, and its performance was largely insensitive to changes in the SMES controller gain parameters. A performance evaluation comparing control schemes that employ adaptive and nonadaptive controllers in the main AGC and SMES control loops is presented. The performance gains produced by the adaptive scheme were especially pronounced for load changes with random magnitude and duration.

We introduce a simple theoretical framework for the reversible magnetization of type-II superconductors as a function of the applied field H, valid throughout the entire field interval between Hc1 and Hc2. In the limit H ≃ Hc1 the framework reduces to a variational model from which Hc1 can be computed accurately in the Ginzburg-Landau regime. In the free-energy evaluation we retain, beyond the supercurrent kinetic energy and the magnetic-field energy, the kinetic-energy and condensation-energy contributions that arise from suppression of the order parameter inside the vortex core. The model is generalized to anisotropic materials by the inclusion of an effective-mass tensor for the situation in which H is parallel to one of the principal axes. The resulting theory is compared with reversible-magnetization measurements on a YBa2Cu3O7 single crystal. The approach allows precise determination of Hc2(T) from magnetization-versus-temperature data taken at fixed applied fields and accounts for the observed field-dependent slopes of those measurements, in contrast to the slope expected from the linear Abrikosov result near Hc2. From this analysis we obtain dHc2/dT = (−1.65 ± 0.23) T/K for H ∥ c near Tc, which corresponds to ξab(0) = (17 ± 1) Å.

In the limit H≃Hc1 the theory reduces to a variational model, from which Hc1 can be accurately computed in the Ginzburg-Landau regime. In calculating the free energy we include, in addition to the supercurrent kinetic energy and the magnetic-field energy, the kinetic-energy and the condensation-energy terms that result from suppression of the order parameter in the vortex core. The model is extended to account for anisotropy by introducing an effective-mass tensor for the case when H is parallel to one of the principal axes. We compare the theory with reversible-magnetization data on a YBa2Cu3O7 single crystal. The method allows an accurate determination of Hc2 versus temperature from measurements of the magnetization versus temperature at fixed magnetic field and explains why the measurements have different slopes in different fields, contrary to what might have been expected from the linear Abrikosov formula near Hc2. The deduced dHc2/dT is (-1.65±0.23) T/K for H parallel to the c axis near Tc, implying ξab(0)=(17±1) Å.

Magnetic studies of flux creep in high temperature superconductors reveal many features that are unexpected within the conventional Anderson-Kim theory, which assumes a thermal activation mechanism and uncorrelated motion of vortex bundles. An alternative phenomenology is provided by the more recent collective pinning theories. Experiments probing this new phenomenology are reviewed, including long-time relaxation, flux-creep annealing and studies near Tc.

A different phenomenology is offered by the more recent collective pinning theories. Experiments probing this new phenomenology are reviewed, including long-time relaxation, flux-creep annealing and studies near Tc.

Using a custom-made dc superconducting quantum interference device (dc-SQUID) magnetometer, we have measured the time relaxation of the remanent magnetization Mrem of a Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ single crystal prepared in the fully critical state for temperatures 13 K ≲ T ≲ 83 K. The experiments span a time window of seven decades, 10^-2 s ≲ t ≲ 10^5 s, allowing the current density j to be probed from values very near jc down to values well below jc. From these measurements we extract (i) the flux-creep activation barriers U(j) as a function of j, (ii) the current–voltage characteristics E(j) in the typical range 10^-7–10^-15 V/cm, and (iii) the zero-temperature critical current density jc(0). Three distinct regimes of vortex dynamics are identified: for T ≲ 20 K the activation barrier U(j) is logarithmic; in the intermediate interval 20 K ≲ T ≲ 40 K no single functional form U(j) could be determined; and for T ≳ 40 K U(j) follows a power-law U(j) ∝ j^-μ with μ ≃ 0.6. Interpreting the data within the weak collective pinning framework for strongly layered superconductors, we argue that for T ≲ 20 K pancake vortices are pinned individually, whereas for T ≳ 40 K pinning involves large collectively pinned vortex bundles. The vortex dynamics in the intermediate temperature range 20 K ≲ T ≲ 40 K are discussed using a qualitative low-field phase diagram of the vortex state in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ; within this picture a second peak in the magnetization loop is expected for 20–40 K, consistent with several magnetization measurements reported in the literature.

From these data we obtained (i) the flux-creep activation barriers U as a function of current density j, (ii) the current–voltage characteristics E(j) in a typical range of 10^-7–10^-15 V/cm, and (iii) the zero-temperature critical current density jc(0). Interpreting the results within the weak collective pinning theory for strongly layered superconductors, we argue that for T ≲ 20 K pancake vortices are pinned individually, whereas for T ≳ 40 K pinning involves large, collectively pinned vortex bundles. The vortex dynamics in the intermediate range 20 K ≲ T ≲ 40 K are discussed on the basis of a qualitative low-field phase diagram of the vortex state in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ; within this picture a second peak in the magnetization loop is expected for temperatures between 20 and 40 K, consistent with observations reported in several magnetization measurements in the literature.

We employed a custom-made dc superconducting quantum interference device (dc-SQUID) magnetometer to record the time relaxation of the remanent magnetization Mrem of a Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ single crystal, starting from the fully critical state, for temperatures in the range 13 K ≲ T ≲ 83 K. The measurements span seven decades in time (10^-2 s ≲ t ≲ 10^5 s), allowing the current density j to be probed from values very close to jc down to substantially smaller than jc. From these data we extracted (i) the flux-creep activation barriers U as a function of current density j, (ii) the current–voltage characteristics E(j) in a typical range 10^-7–10^-15 V/cm, and (iii) the zero-temperature critical current density jc(0). Three distinct vortex-dynamic regimes are found: for T ≲ 20 K the activation barrier U(j) is logarithmic; in the intermediate interval 20 K ≲ T ≲ 40 K no single functional form U(j) could be identified; and for T ≳ 40 K U(j) follows a power-law with exponent μ ≃ 0.6. Interpreting the results within the weak collective pinning theory for strongly layered superconductors, we argue that for T ≲ 20 K pancake vortices are pinned individually, whereas for T ≳ 40 K pinning involves large, collectively pinned vortex bundles. A qualitative low-field phase diagram of the vortex state in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ provides a basis for describing the vortex dynamics in the intermediate-temperature interval 20 K ≲ T ≲ 40 K; within this picture a second peak in the magnetization loop is expected for 20–40 K, consistent with several magnetization measurements reported in the literature.

We examine the frequency-dependent photoinduced infrared conductivity, σP(ω), extracted from photoinduced absorption measurements on the insulators Tl2Ba2Ca0.98Gd0.02Cu2O8, YBa2Cu3O6.3, and La2CuO4, by comparing it to σPT(ω) computed using nonadiabatic polaron-transport theory. The computed σPT(ω) reproduces the experimental σP(ω) well in the midinfrared. We further contrast σP(ω) with the infrared conductivity, σ(ω), of the high-Tc superconductors Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8, YBa2Cu3O7, and La1.85Sr0.15CuO4. The common spectral form and systematic parallels observed in both σP(ω) and σ(ω) suggest that carriers in the concentrated (metallic) regime preserve much of the character of carriers in the dilute (photoexcited) regime. Taken together, these findings imply that in the superconducting cuprates and in their "parent" insulators the carriers are polarons, each dressed by a phonon polarization cloud.

The development of modern science and technology requires high magnetic fields exceeding 25T. Second-generation high-temperature superconducting wires, i.e. REBCO (REBa<sub>2</sub>Cu<sub>3</sub>O<sub>7</sub>-x, RE refers to Y, Gd, Dy, Eu and other rare-earth elements) coated conductors (CCs), have emerged as the preferred choice for constructing high-field magnets owing to their high irreversible magnetic field. Mechanical stresses arising from manufacturing processes, thermal mismatch and Lorenz forces strongly affect the electromagnetic performance of REBCO CCs during operation. In addition, recently studied screen currents influence the mechanical characteristics of high-field REBCO magnets. In this review, the principal experimental and theoretical studies concerning critical current degradation, delamination and fatigue, and shear investigations on REBCO CCs are examined first. Then, research progress on the screening-current effect in the development of high-field superconducting magnets is presented, and finally the key mechanical problems facing the future development of high-field magnets based on REBCO CCs are prospected.

In conventional BCS superconductors, the quantum condensation of superconducting electron pairs is viewed as a Fermi-surface instability, in which attractive interactions pair the low-energy electrons. Whether this description remains applicable to high-Tc materials such as the cuprates and iron-based superconductors is still unresolved. In particular, an alternative picture in which electron pairs are formed locally by repulsive interactions may instead prevail. Here we present a high-resolution angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy study of LiFe1−xCoxAs. We observe a large and robust superconducting gap on a band that sinks below the Fermi level upon Co substitution. The detected Fermi-surface-free superconducting order is also the largest across momentum space, excluding a proximity-effect origin and indicating that the order parameter is not tied to the Fermi surface as a consequence of a surface instability.

In conventional BCS superconductors the quantum condensation of superconducting electron pairs is described as a Fermi-surface instability, where low-energy electrons are paired by attractive interactions. Whether this framework remains applicable to high-Tc materials such as the cuprates and iron-based superconductors is still unresolved. In particular, an alternative, fundamentally different picture—in which electron pairs form locally through repulsive interactions—has been proposed. Here we present a high-resolution angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy study of LiFe1−xCoxAs. We find a large, robust superconducting gap on a band that, upon Co substitution, sinks below the Fermi level. This Fermi-surface-free superconducting order exhibits the largest gap across momentum space, excluding a proximity-effect origin and demonstrating that the order parameter is not tied to the Fermi surface due to a surface instability.

In condensed-matter systems, raising the temperature usually suppresses ordered states, producing an upper critical temperature for phenomena such as magnetism and superconductivity. One notable exception is the Pomeranchuk effect in 3He, where the liquid ground state freezes as temperature increases1 because the paramagnetic solid phase has larger entropy. We demonstrate that an analogous mechanism governs the finite-temperature behavior of spin and valley isospins in magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene2. In particular, a peak in the resistivity appears at elevated temperatures near the superlattice filling factor of −1, even though no evidence of a commensurate correlated phase emerges in the low-temperature limit. Measurements of tilted-field magnetotransport and thermodynamic probes of the in-plane magnetic moment link this resistivity maximum to a finite-field magnetic phase transition3 at which the system acquires a nonzero isospin polarization. These observations are consistent with a Pomeranchuk-type scenario: the entropy associated with disordered isospin moments within the ferromagnetic phase stabilizes that phase relative to an isospin-unpolarized Fermi-liquid phase at higher temperatures. We estimate the entropy to be of order unity per unit cell area in units of Boltzmann’s constant, and a measurable portion of this entropy is suppressed by an in-plane magnetic field, in line with a contribution from disordered spins. Unlike 3He, however, we do not observe thermodynamic discontinuities across this transition. Our results point to a small isospin stiffness4,5 and carry implications for the character of finite-temperature electron transport6–8 as well as for the mechanisms responsible for isospin ordering and superconductivity9,10 in twisted bilayer graphene and related materials.

In condensed-matter systems, ordered phases are generally suppressed as temperature increases, producing an upper critical temperature for phenomena such as magnetism and superconductivity. An exception is the Pomeranchuk effect in 3He1, where heating can drive the liquid ground state into a solid because the paramagnetic solid carries a larger entropy. Here we demonstrate that an analogous mechanism governs the finite-temperature behaviour of spin and valley isospins in magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene2. Specifically, a high-temperature resistivity peak emerges near a superlattice filling factor of −1 even though no evidence of a commensurate correlated phase appears in the low-temperature limit. Measurements of tilted-field magnetotransport together with thermodynamic determinations of the in-plane magnetic moment indicate that this resistivity peak is tied to a finite-field magnetic phase transition3 at which the system acquires a nonzero isospin polarization. The results are consistent with a Pomeranchuk-type scenario: the entropy associated with disordered isospin moments in the ferromagnetic state stabilizes that state relative to an isospin-unpolarized Fermi liquid at higher temperatures. We quantify the entropy (in units of Boltzmann’s constant) to be on the order of unity per unit cell area, and observe a measurable portion that is reduced by an in-plane magnetic field in a manner consistent with contributions from disordered spins. Unlike in 3He, no thermodynamic discontinuities are detected across this transition. These observations point to a small isospin stiffness4,5 and have consequences for finite-temperature electron transport6–8 and for the mechanisms responsible for isospin ordering and superconductivity9,10 in twisted bilayer graphene and related materials.

We report ferromagnetism with Curie temperature Tc = 230 K in the new diluted magnetic semiconductor (Ba0.7K0.3)(Zn0.85Mn0.15)2As2, isostructural with the ferropnictide 122 superconductors and synthesized by low-temperature sintering. In this DMS, spin is introduced by isovalent substitution of Mn2+ for Zn2+, while charge is supplied by heterovalent substitution of K1+ for Ba2+ in (Ba0.7K0.3)(Zn0.85Mn0.15)2As2, a scheme that differs from (Ga,Mn)As where both spin and charge are induced simultaneously by heterovalent substitution of Mn2+ for Ga3+. The (Ba0.7K0.3)(Zn0.85Mn0.15)2As2 sample shows spontaneous magnetization following a T3/2 dependence expected for a homogeneous ferromagnet, with a saturation moment of 1.0 μB per Mn atom.

We examine the connection between phonon anomalies and high superconducting temperature Tc in transition metals and their carbides within the framework of strong-coupling theory. Using the previously developed model theory for the lattice dynamics of these materials, we analyze how the electron-phonon coupling constant λ, which essentially determines Tc, depends on the phonon frequencies ω. We find that the change in the moment 〈 ω2 〉 produced by the anomalies is insufficient to explain the variation of λ. This result is interpreted by assuming that the electron-phonon matrix elements entering λ are determined not only by a p-d contact term but also rather strongly by d-d overlap interaction.

We examine the relationship between phonon anomalies and elevated superconducting temperature Tc in transition metals and their carbides within the framework of strong-coupling theory. Employing the model previously developed for the lattice dynamics of these compounds, we study how the electron–phonon coupling constant λ depends on the phonon frequencies ω; λ being the principal determinant of Tc. We show that the change in the moment 〈 ω2 〉 induced by the anomalies is insufficient to account for the observed variation in λ. This finding is interpreted by positing that the electron–phonon matrix elements entering λ are governed not solely by a p–d contact term but are also appreciably influenced by d–d overlap interactions.
