Magnetic impurities strongly influence superconductivity by breaking time-reversal symmetry and creating Yu-Shiba-Rusinov (YSR) low energy bound states within the superconducting gap. The spatial reach of YSR states is greatly increased in two-dimensional (2D) systems, which enhances the likelihood of forming coupled states. Here, we report observation of YSR states on single cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPC) molecules on the 2D superconductor NbSe2 using low-temperature scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and spectroscopy. Using STM lateral manipulation to assemble controlled CoPc dimers, we demonstrate the emergence of coupled YSR states. The experimental findings are corroborated by theoretical analysis of the coupled states within lattice and continuum models.

Magnetic impurities strongly affect superconductivity by breaking time-reversal symmetry and producing Yu-Shiba-Rusinov (YSR) low-energy bound states inside the superconducting gap. In two-dimensional (2D) materials the spatial extent of YSR states is substantially increased, which should promote the formation of coupled states. In this work we detect YSR states on individual cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPC) molecules adsorbed on the 2D superconductor NbSe2 using low-temperature scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and spectroscopy. By employing STM lateral manipulation we assemble controlled CoPc dimers and demonstrate the emergence of coupled YSR states. The experimental observations are supported by theoretical analysis of the coupled states within both lattice and continuum models.

By employing STM lateral manipulation to assemble controlled CoPc dimers, we demonstrate the formation of coupled YSR states. The experimental findings are supported by theoretical analysis of the coupled states in both lattice and continuum models.

Magnetic impurities strongly affect superconductivity by breaking time-reversal symmetry and creating the so-called Yu-Shiba-Rusinov (YSR) low energy bound states inside the superconducting gap. In two-dimensional (2D) materials the spatial extent of YSR states is significantly enlarged, which promotes the formation of coupled states. In this work we detect YSR states on individual cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPC) molecules deposited on the 2D superconductor NbSe2 using low-temperature scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and spectroscopy. By employing STM lateral manipulation we assemble controlled CoPc dimers and observe the emergence of coupled YSR states. The experimental observations are corroborated by theoretical analysis of the coupled states using both lattice and continuum models.

By employing STM lateral manipulation to assemble controlled CoPc dimers, we directly demonstrate the emergence of coupled YSR states. The experimental findings are supported by theoretical analysis of the coupled states using both lattice and continuum models.

A key ingredient for constructing a successful theoretical description of high-temperature superconductivity is knowledge of the symmetry of the order parameter (or wavefunction) that represents electron pairing in the superconducting phase. Several experimental investigations1–8 have produced persuasive evidence for an anisotropic order parameter consistent with dx2–y2 symmetry. However, none of these earlier measurements could categorically exclude an additional isotropic s-wave component; they either examined materials with an orthorhombic crystal structure—where a mixed s + d state is increasingly viewed as a likely outcome9,10—or depended on detailed modelling to interpret effects of disorder and defects that remain uncertain. We present here results from an experiment specifically designed to avoid these complications: the compound studied is the single-layer tetragonal superconductor Tl2Ba2CuO6+δ, and the geometry of the measurement is chosen so that conclusions rest solely on symmetry considerations. Our findings show that this material exhibits pure dx2–y2 pairing symmetry, thereby providing a clear reference point from which to approach the more complex mixed s + d states seen in other high-temperature superconductors.

The detection of replica bands in single-unit-cell FeSe on SrTiO3 (STO)(001) by angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) prompted the proposal that coupling between FeSe electrons and the STO phonons underlies the enhancement of Tc relative to other FeSe-based superconductors. The more recent report of a comparable superconducting gap in single-unit-cell FeSe/STO(110) therefore raised the question of whether the same mechanism operates there. In this work we present an ARPES investigation of the electronic structure of FeSe/STO(110). As in FeSe/STO(001), we observe distinct replica bands. In addition, we compare the bare STO(001) and STO(110) surfaces and find similar replica bands on both, separated by approximately the same energy, which indicates that this coupling is a generic characteristic of STO surfaces and interfaces. These results imply that the large superconducting gaps seen in FeSe films on different STO surface terminations are likely enhanced by a common mechanism.

Efforts to realize artificial topological superconductivity have been hampered by the stringent conditions necessary for its emergence. Recent discoveries of diverse correlated electronic states in twisted van der Waals materials illustrate that moiré patterns serve as a powerful knob for engineering artificial electronic structures. In this work we demonstrate that a moiré pattern formed between a van der Waals superconductor and a monolayer ferromagnet produces a periodic potential modulation which permits realization of a topological superconducting state that would be inaccessible without the moiré. The magnetic moiré generates Yu-Shiba-Rusinov minibands and imposes a periodic modulation on Majorana edge modes; we detect these features using low-temperature scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and spectroscopy (STS). Moiré patterns, and more generally periodic potential modulations, therefore provide a powerful route to overcome conventional constraints on realizing and controlling topological superconductivity.

As exemplified by recent observations of diverse correlated electronic states in twisted van der Waals materials, moiré patterns can serve as a powerful tuning knob to create artificial electronic structures. Here we demonstrate that a moiré pattern formed between a van der Waals superconductor and a monolayer ferromagnet produces a periodic potential modulation that enables a topological superconducting state that would not be accessible in the absence of the moiré. The magnetic moiré pattern generates Yu-Shiba-Rusinov minibands and a periodic modulation of the Majorana edge modes, which we detect using low-temperature scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and spectroscopy (STS). Moiré patterns and, more broadly, periodic potential modulations therefore constitute powerful means to overcome conventional constraints for realizing and controlling topological superconductivity.

This paper presents general considerations for selecting a cryogenic cooling system for superconducting machines across different applications, examined with respect to design, operation, and condition-monitoring constraints. The discussion is intended to familiarize electric machine engineers and designers with the cryogenic aspects relevant to choosing and employing cooling systems for superconducting machines. Central questions addressed are: which factors are most important to consider when selecting a cooling system for a superconducting machine application, and which of those factors can subsequently influence the cooling-system performance so that it can handle the expected heat load without experiencing thermal breakdown? To answer these questions, common cooling-system architectures, various cryogenic fluids, related parameters tied to heat-load magnitudes, and appropriate safety margins are reviewed to assist machine engineers in selecting the cryogenic cooling solution that best matches their superconducting machine and its specific requirements. Considerations for auxiliary components, such as heat exchangers and pumps, are also examined as the next priority in cooling-system selection. Special challenges and constraints imposed on cooling systems for superconducting machines in aerospace contexts—such as electric- and hydrogen-powered aircraft—naval applications, and wind-energy installations are discussed as well. While these applications share concerns like weight, cost, and specific mass of cooling systems, each application also carries particular concerns and constraints that take precedence.

Density wave (DW) order is thought to be linked to superconductivity in the recently discovered high-temperature superconductor La3Ni2O7. Nevertheless, studies probing how this order evolves under high pressure remain scarce. Here we investigate quasiparticle dynamics in bilayer nickelate La3Ni2O7 single crystals using ultrafast optical pump-probe spectroscopy at pressures up to 34.2 GPa. Under ambient conditions, the temperature-dependent relaxation traces exhibit a phonon-bottleneck signature arising from the opening of an energy gap near 151 K. Analysis with the Rothwarf–Taylor model assigns an energy scale of 66 meV to this DW-like gap. Combined with recent experimental results, we propose that the DW-like transition at ambient pressure and low temperature corresponds to a spin density wave (SDW). With increasing pressure the SDW is strongly suppressed up to 13.3 GPa and disappears entirely by roughly 26 GPa. Notably, above 29.4 GPa we observe the emergence of a different DW-like order with a transition temperature of about 135 K, likely related to the predicted charge density wave (CDW). These results provide experimental evidence for the pressure-driven evolution of the DW-like gap and offer critical insights into the connection between DW order and superconductivity in La3Ni2O7.

It is demonstrated that, under certain conditions, a superconductor with spin fluctuations (paramagnons) can exhibit identical tunneling characteristics and thermodynamic properties to a superconductor without paramagnons that has the same electron-phonon interaction but scaled down and with an increased effective Coulomb interaction. The condition required is that the paramagnon frequency be much higher than any phonon frequency. The effective anisotropy in the electron-phonon interaction is also shown to increase.

We show that, under certain conditions, a superconductor containing spin fluctuations (paramagnons) displays the same tunneling characteristics and thermodynamic properties as a superconductor without paramagnons but with the identical electron-phonon interaction reduced in amplitude and with an increased effective Coulomb interaction. The required condition is that the paramagnon frequency be much larger than any phonon frequency. It is also demonstrated that the effective anisotropy of the electron-phonon interaction increases.

We show that, under certain conditions, a superconductor with spin fluctuations (paramagnons) can exhibit the same tunneling characteristics and thermodynamic properties as a superconductor without paramagnons, provided the electron-phonon interaction is identical but scaled down and the effective Coulomb interaction is increased. The necessary condition is that the paramagnon frequency be much higher than any phonon frequency. An increase in the effective anisotropy of the electron-phonon interaction is also demonstrated.

When the paramagnon frequency is much larger than any phonon frequency, a superconductor with spin fluctuations (paramagnons) is shown to display the same tunneling characteristics and thermodynamic properties as a superconductor lacking paramagnons but possessing the same electron-phonon interaction scaled down and an increased effective Coulomb interaction. The required condition is that the paramagnon frequency exceed all phonon frequencies by a large margin. The analysis also demonstrates an increase in the effective anisotropy of the electron-phonon interaction.

We show that when the paramagnon frequency is much higher than any phonon frequency, a superconductor with spin fluctuations (paramagnons) can exhibit the same tunneling characteristics and thermodynamic properties as a superconductor without paramagnons whose electron-phonon interaction is the same but scaled down and which possesses an increased effective Coulomb interaction. Under these conditions the effective anisotropy in the electron-phonon interaction is also shown to increase.

Determining whether a quantum critical point (QCP) lies beneath the superconducting dome has been a long-standing question that remains unresolved in many families of unconventional superconductors, notably cuprates, heavy fermions, and, most recently, iron pnictides. The presence of a QCP could provide a pathway to explaining the origin of unconventional superconductors’ anomalous non-Fermi liquid properties, the microscopic coexistence between unconventional superconductivity and magnetic or some other exotic order, and, ultimately, the mechanism of superconductivity itself. The isovalent substituted iron pnictide BaFe2(As1−xPx)2 provides a new platform for the study of quantum criticality, offering a unique opportunity to follow the evolution of electronic properties across a wide region of the phase diagram. Recent experiments in BaFe2(As1−xPx)2 have yielded the first clear and unambiguous evidence of a second-order quantum phase transition lying beneath the superconducting dome.

Ni-doped ZnO (Zn1−xNixO, 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.05) diluted magnetic semiconductor nanoparticles were synthesized via an ultrasonic-assisted sol–gel process. Transmission electron microscopy reveals sphere-like nanoparticles with an average size of about 25 nm. X-ray diffraction analysis identifies the Ni-doped ZnO nanoparticles as having a wurtzite structure, although impurity phases appear once the Ni content reaches x = 0.05. Raman spectra further probe the sample structures, revealing a broad and strong Raman band in the 500–600 cm−1 range in Zn1−xNixO, and showing that the wurtzite structure degrades gradually as x increases. Magnetic measurements performed at room temperature using a superconducting quantum interference device indicate that Zn1−xNixO nanoparticles with x ≤ 0.02 are ferromagnetic, whereas the Zn0.95Ni0.05O sample exhibits paramagnetism, which may be ascribed to ferromagnetic–antiferromagnetic competition.

Ni-doped ZnO (Zn1−xNixO, 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.05) diluted magnetic semiconductor nanoparticles were synthesized by an ultrasonic-assisted sol–gel process. Transmission electron microscopy revealed sphere-like particles with an average size of about 25 nm. X-ray diffraction analysis identifies the Ni-doped ZnO nanoparticles as having the wurtzite structure, although impurity phases are detected when the Ni content reaches x = 0.05. Raman spectra further characterize the samples, showing a broad, strong band in the 500–600 cm−1 range for Zn1−xNixO and indicating that the wurtzite structure progressively degrades as x increases. Room-temperature magnetic measurements using a superconducting quantum interference device show ferromagnetism for Zn1−xNixO with x ≤ 0.02, whereas the Zn0.95Ni0.05O sample displays paramagnetism, which may be ascribed to ferromagnetic–antiferromagnetic competition.

We use angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy to report the direct observation of an energy gap in 2H-NbSe2 caused by the charge-density waves (CDW). The gap opens in regions of the momentum space connected by the CDW vectors, which implies a nesting mechanism of CDW formation. In remarkable analogy with the pseudogap in cuprates, the detected energy gap also exists in the normal state (T>T0) where it breaks the Fermi surface into "arcs"; it is nonmonotonic as a function of temperature with a local minimum at the CDW transition temperature (T0), and it forestalls the superconducting gap by excluding the nested portions of the Fermi surface from participating in superconductivity.

We present an order-αs calculation of pp(-)→W-W++X. Results are provided for the total cross section and for differential distributions at Fermilab Tevatron, CERN LHC, and Superconducting SuperCollider energies. The calculation uses a combination of analytic and Monte Carlo integration methods, which makes it straightforward to compute a variety of observables and to impose experimental cuts.

This review describes the principal normal- and superconducting-state properties of magnesium diboride, a material known since the early 1950s but only recently found to be superconductive with a remarkably high critical temperature Tc = 40 K for a binary compound. What distinguishes MgB2? Its elevated Tc, simple crystal structure, large coherence lengths, high critical current densities and critical fields, together with grain boundaries that are transparent to current, suggest MgB2 is a promising candidate for both large-scale applications and electronic devices. Over the past seven months MgB2 has been produced in multiple forms — bulk, single crystals, thin films, tapes and wires. The highest critical current densities (greater than 10 MA cm−2) and critical fields (40 T) have been obtained in thin films. The anisotropy ratio γ inferred from upper critical field measurements remains unresolved, since reported values span a wide range, γ = 1.2–9. Likewise, there is no consensus on whether a single anisotropic energy gap or a double gap exists. A central question is whether MgB2 constitutes a new class of superconductors, potentially the tip of an iceberg of similar materials waiting to be discovered. To date MgB2 retains the record for the highest Tc among simple binary compounds. Nevertheless, the discovery of superconductivity in MgB2 has revived interest in non-oxides and spurred searches for superconductivity in related materials; several compounds have since been reported superconductive: TaB2, BeB2.75, C–S composites, and elemental B under pressure.

This review summarizes the principal normal- and superconducting-state characteristics of magnesium diboride (MgB2), a material known since the early 1950s but only recently identified as superconducting with a remarkably high critical temperature Tc = 40 K for a binary compound. MgB2’s combination of a high Tc, a simple crystal structure, large coherence lengths, high critical current densities and critical fields, and grain boundaries that are transparent to current suggests it could be an excellent candidate for both large-scale applications and electronic devices. Over the past seven months MgB2 has been produced in many forms — bulk, single crystals, thin films, tapes and wires — and the highest critical current densities (>10 MA cm−2) and largest critical fields (40 T) have been realized in thin films. The anisotropy ratio deduced from upper critical field measurements remains unresolved, with reported values spanning γ = 1.2–9, and there is no consensus yet on whether a single anisotropic gap or two distinct energy gaps exist. A central question is whether MgB2 defines a new class of superconductors, potentially the tip of an iceberg awaiting discovery. To date MgB2 still holds the record for the highest Tc among simple binary compounds, and its discovery has renewed interest in non-oxide materials and sparked searches for superconductivity in related systems; several compounds have since been reported superconducting, including TaB2, BeB2.75, C–S composites, and elemental boron under pressure.

When the continuous symmetry of a physical system is spontaneously broken, two collective excitations generally arise: the amplitude and phase modes of the order-parameter fluctuations. In superconductors, the amplitude excitation is most recently termed the Higgs mode, as it represents a condensed-matter analog of the Higgs boson in particle physics. The Higgs mode is a scalar oscillation of the order parameter, distinct from charge or spin fluctuations, and therefore does not couple linearly to electromagnetic fields. This absence of linear electromagnetic coupling is why the Higgs mode in superconductors escaped experimental observation for over a half century after its initial theoretical prediction, except in systems where a charge-density-wave coexists. With the development of nonlinear and time-resolved terahertz spectroscopy techniques, however, it has become possible to probe the Higgs mode through nonlinear light–Higgs coupling. In this review, we overview recent progress in the study of the Higgs mode in superconductors.

This lack of linear coupling explains why the Higgs mode in superconductors escaped experimental observation for over a half century after the initial theoretical prediction, except for a charge-density-wave coexisting system. With advances in nonlinear and time-resolved terahertz spectroscopy techniques, however, it has become possible to study the Higgs mode via the nonlinear light–Higgs coupling. In this review, we overview recent progress in the study of the Higgs mode in superconductors.

When a continuous symmetry of a physical system is broken spontaneously, two collective excitations generally appear: the amplitude and phase modes associated with fluctuations of the order parameter. In superconductors, the amplitude mode has more recently been termed the Higgs mode, as it constitutes a condensed-matter analog of the Higgs boson known from particle physics. The Higgs mode is a scalar excitation of the order parameter, separate from charge or spin fluctuations, and therefore it does not couple linearly to electromagnetic fields. For this reason, the Higgs mode in superconductors remained experimentally inaccessible for over half a century after its original theoretical prediction, with the lone exception of systems in which a charge-density-wave coexists. Advances in nonlinear and time-resolved terahertz spectroscopy, however, have enabled exploration of the Higgs mode via nonlinear light–Higgs coupling. In this review, we survey recent developments in the study of the Higgs mode in superconductors.

When a continuous symmetry of a physical system is spontaneously broken, two collective excitations typically appear: amplitude and phase modes of the order-parameter fluctuation. In superconductors, the amplitude excitation has come to be called the Higgs mode, as it provides a condensed-matter analogue of the Higgs boson in particle physics. The Higgs mode is a scalar fluctuation of the order parameter, different from charge or spin excitations, and therefore it does not couple linearly to electromagnetic fields. For this reason, aside from cases where a charge-density-wave coexists, the Higgs mode in superconductors eluded experimental detection for more than half a century after its initial theoretical prediction. With the development of nonlinear, time-resolved terahertz spectroscopy, however, it has become possible to probe the Higgs mode via nonlinear light–Higgs coupling. In this review, we survey recent progress in investigations of the Higgs mode in superconductors.

Since interface superconductivity was discovered at the junction between the two insulating oxide layers LaAlO<sub>3</sub> and SrTiO<sub>3</sub>, systems in which superconductivity is induced by interfaces have become a major research focus. Here we report homogeneous interfaces produced by randomly stacking chemically exfoliated monolayer TaS<sub>2</sub> nanosheets. The restacked material exhibits enhanced superconductivity with T<sub>c</sub> = 3 K, compared with 0.8 K for the parent 2H-TaS<sub>2</sub>. Heat-capacity measurements reveal an increased electronic specific-heat coefficient γ in the restacked TaS<sub>2</sub> nanosheets relative to parent 2H-TaS<sub>2</sub> crystals. Density functional theory calculations indicate that an increase and delocalization of electronic states near the Fermi surface, arising from the homogeneous-interface effects, could account for the enhanced superconductivity.

Density functional theory calculations suggest that an increase and delocalization of electronic states near the Fermi surface, arising from the effects of the homogeneous interfaces, could account for the enhanced superconductivity.

Understanding the normal-state and superconducting behavior of iron-based superconductors requires sensible microscopic models. Since these materials are largely metallic, an appropriate starting point is an effective low-energy description that captures the electronic states near the Fermi level and their interactions. Unlike cuprates, iron-based high-Tc compounds are multi-orbital systems subject to Hubbard and Hund interactions, which lead to a complex 10-orbital lattice model. In this review we survey various minimal models proposed to extract the universal aspects of these systems. We begin with minimal models formulated purely in the orbital basis, which target a selected orbital subspace, and with models formulated purely in the band basis, which rely only on the Fermi-surface geometry. Orbital-basis minimal models provide valuable qualitative insight into the role of orbital degrees of freedom but do not separate high-energy from low-energy sectors and therefore typically remain at the mean-field level. Band-basis minimal models permit analyses beyond mean-field and enable studies of the interplay between superconducting and magnetic orders and of Ising-nematic order, yet they fail to describe orbital-dependent phenomena such as spontaneous orbital order. We then review recent proposals for a minimal model built in the band basis that nonetheless fully incorporates the orbital composition and symmetry properties of the low-energy excitations. We discuss renormalization-group results for this model, focusing on the competition and coexistence of superconductivity, magnetism, and spontaneous orbital order, and compare the theoretical outcomes with experiments on iron pnictides and chalcogenides. Finally, we consider the consequences of glide-plane symmetry for low-energy models, emphasizing the central role of spin-orbit coupling.

Developing realistic microscopic models is crucial to understand both the normal-state and superconducting behavior of the iron-based superconductors. Since these materials are largely metallic, an appropriate starting point is an effective low-energy description that captures the electronic states in the vicinity of the Fermi level and their mutual interactions. Unlike the cuprates, however, iron-based high-Tc compounds are intrinsically multi-orbital, subject to Hubbard and Hund interactions, which leads to a rather complex 10-orbital lattice Hamiltonian. In this review we examine several proposed minimal models designed to expose the universal aspects of these systems. We begin with minimal formulations expressed purely in the orbital basis, which concentrate on a selected orbital subspace, and with formulations defined purely in the band basis, which depend only on the Fermi-surface geometry. Orbital-basis minimal models yield important qualitative understanding of the role of orbital degrees of freedom but do not separate high- and low-energy sectors and therefore typically remain at the mean-field level. Band-basis minimal models permit analyses beyond mean-field, allowing one to study the competition and coexistence of superconducting and magnetic states as well as Ising-nematic order, yet they are unable to describe orbital-specific phenomena such as spontaneous orbital order. We then survey recent minimal-model proposals formulated in the band basis that nonetheless fully incorporate the orbital composition and symmetry properties of the low-energy excitations. We discuss renormalization-group results for such models, focusing on the interplay among superconductivity, magnetism, and spontaneous orbital order, and we compare the theoretical predictions with experiments on iron pnictides and chalcogenides. Finally, we address how the glide-plane symmetry affects low-energy descriptions and emphasize the central role of spin-orbit coupling.

Constructing physically reasonable microscopic models is crucial for understanding both the normal-state and superconducting behavior of the iron-based superconductors. Given that these materials are predominantly metallic, a natural starting point is an effective low-energy description that captures the electronic states around the Fermi level and their mutual interactions. Unlike the cuprates, however, iron-based high-Tc compounds are multi-orbital systems governed by Hubbard and Hund interactions, which in microscopic detail lead to a complex 10-orbital lattice model. In this review we survey various minimal models that have been advanced to reveal the universal aspects of these systems. We begin with minimal descriptions formulated purely in the orbital basis, which concentrate on a selected orbital subspace, and with models formulated purely in the band basis, which are built from the Fermi-surface geometry alone. Orbital-basis minimal models yield valuable qualitative understanding of the role played by orbital degrees of freedom but do not separate high- and low-energy sectors and, consequently, typically remain at the mean-field level. Band-basis minimal models permit treatments beyond mean field and can probe the competition and coexistence of superconducting and magnetic orders as well as Ising-nematic order, yet they fail to capture orbital-dependent phenomena such as spontaneous orbital order. We next review recent proposals for a minimal model formulated in the band basis that nevertheless fully retains the orbital composition and symmetry constraints of the low-energy excitations. We discuss renormalization-group results for this model, with emphasis on the interplay among superconductivity, magnetism, and spontaneous orbital order, and we compare the theoretical predictions with experiments on iron pnictides and chalcogenides. Finally, we examine the consequences of the glide-plane symmetry for low-energy descriptions and emphasize the central role of spin-orbit coupling.
